name: Waikiki
desc: Tourist epicenter of Hawaii — packed high-rises, legendary beach, world-class dining, but feels like a resort, not a neighborhood.
insider: Fort DeRussy Beach is the widest, least crowded stretch of Waikiki sand — locals go here to avoid the tourist crush
Marukame Udon has a long line but it moves in 10 minutes — the fresh tempura udon for $7 is the best cheap meal in Waikiki
The backstreets behind Kuhio Ave have cheap local food — Waikiki Yokocho food hall and Musubi Cafe Iyasume for spam musubi
Duke's on Sunday morning for breakfast is when the crowd is thinnest and the oceanfront vibe is best
Parking in Waikiki costs $30-50/day at hotels — park free at Kapiolani Park or Ala Wai Canal and walk in
The Royal Hawaiian (Pink Palace) opened in 1927 and you can walk through the lobby and grounds for free — old glamour Hawaii
Waikiki was once a swampy marshland — the Ala Wai Canal was built in the 1920s to drain it for development
International Marketplace has been reborn as an upscale open-air mall — the massive banyan tree at the center is the centerpiece of the redesigned complex
House Without a Key at the Halekulani does sunset hula and cocktails — the most elegant free show in Waikiki
Rent is astronomical — expect $2,000+ for a tiny studio and $3,000+ for a 1BR, and most buildings are old with no laundry
The surf break at Queens is perfect for beginners — longboard rentals on the beach are about $20/hour
